sound_type: "Who cooks for you? Who cooks for you-all?" -- rhythmic 8-note phrase, 400-550 Hz fundamental, rich and hoarse timbre, measured deliberate phrasing; both sexes call; active day and night
ecosystem: Mature forest, resident. Old-growth and mature second-growth. Dense canopy. Water association. Year-round. Expanding from eastern origin into PNW.
season: Year-round resident. Calling peaks during breeding season (February-April) and fall territorial establishment. Calls year-round, day and night.

coordinates.description: Barred Owls now abundant throughout PNW forests, parks, and suburban areas with large trees.

taxonomy_note.description: First Barred Owl entry. Third consecutive Strigidae (after GHO 135-136). The Barred Owl is an eastern species that has expanded westward into the PNW, creating a conservation crisis for the Northern Spotted Owl (SPS degree 4). E=4 reflects measured, rhythmic hooting. The caterwauling (degree 138, E=6) is a separate acoustic event.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Strigidae: fourth entry total (Spotted Owl 4, GHO 135, GHO pair duet 136, Barred Owl 137). Three consecutive at 135-137.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Strix from Greek 'screech owl.' varia from Latin 'variegated,' referring to the barred plumage pattern.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Strix -- first entry for this genus. Different from Bubo (Great Horned Owl). Strix includes both Barred and Spotted Owls.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Strigiformes. Fourth entry. Three consecutive at 135-137.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Strigidae continues for a third consecutive degree. The owl family era deepens.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 400-550 Hz. Significantly higher than GHO (250-400 Hz). Rich, resonant, slightly hoarse quality.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 420-500 Hz. The main vocalization energy is concentrated in this band.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Primary call: 400-550 Hz. Individual variation in exact pitch. Female slightly higher than male.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Single phrase: 3-4 seconds (8 notes). Calling bouts: phrases every 10-20 seconds for several minutes.
song_metrics.note_structure: 8-NOTE PHRASE: 'Who cooks for YOU?' (4 notes, rising emphasis) + 'Who cooks for you-ALL?' (4-5 notes, drawn-out finish). The mnemonic is remarkably accurate to the actual acoustic pattern.
song_metrics.note_types: Territorial hoot (8-note phrase), contact call, location call. The 8-note phrase is the primary vocalization.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Two-part phrase: 4 notes + 4-5 notes with distinct rhythmic pattern and emphasis. Deliberate pacing between phrases (10-20 seconds).
song_metrics.timbre: Rich, resonant, slightly hoarse or 'barking.' Warmer and more textured than GHO. Some describe as nasal or throaty. Distinctive and immediately recognizable.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4. Moderately loud -- carries 200-400 meters through dense forest. Louder and more frequent than Spotted Owl. Less powerful than GHO pair duet (E=5).
song_metrics.tempo: Measured, unhurried. Phrases every 10-20 seconds. Deliberate pacing suggesting territorial confidence.
song_metrics.context: Territorial declaration (primary), contact between mates, breeding season intensification. Calls year-round, day and night -- more diurnal than most owls.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal vocal variation. The 8-note phrase structure is consistent across the species' range. Individual variation in timing and tonal quality.
song_metrics.key_property: "WHO COOKS FOR YOU?" -- E=4 MEASURED MNEMONIC. 8-note rhythmic phrase at 400-550 Hz. Rich, hoarse timbre. Day and night calling. Third consecutive Strigidae (135-137). Eastern species expanding into PNW. Spotted Owl conflict. Fourth Strigidae in engine (after Spotted Owl 4, GHO 135-136).

plumage.overall: Brown and white barred pattern. Rounded head without ear tufts (unlike Great Horned Owl). Dark brown eyes (unlike yellow-eyed GHO). Prominent facial disc with concentric brown and white rings.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Dark eyes (brown, not yellow). No ear tufts. Horizontal barring on chest, vertical streaking on belly. The barred pattern gives the species its common name.
plumage.flight_pattern: Silent flight like other owls. Broad wings, short tail. Moth-like buoyant flight in low-light conditions.

physical_description.overall: Length: 43-50 cm. Weight: 500-1050 g. Wingspan: 99-110 cm. Stocky, rounded body. Large round head without ear tufts. ~20% larger than Northern Spotted Owl.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Dark brown eyes distinguish from yellow-eyed Great Horned Owl and Spotted Owl. Barred chest pattern (horizontal bars) vs. Spotted Owl's spotted pattern (white spots on brown).
physical_description.field_id: Large round-headed owl without ear tufts. Dark eyes. Barred chest. Listen for 'Who cooks for you?' at dusk and dawn. More likely to call during day than GHO.

nodes.title: "Who Cooks for You?" -- E=4 Vocal Mnemonic
nodes.description: The most recognizable owl mnemonic. 8-note rhythmic phrase at 400-550 Hz. Rich, hoarse timbre. Both sexes call. Active day and night. Measured, deliberate phrasing. The mnemonic matches the acoustic pattern with remarkable accuracy.

nodes.title: Spotted Owl Conflict -- Conservation Crisis
nodes.description: Barred Owl displaces Northern Spotted Owl through competitive exclusion. 20% larger body, broader diet, wider habitat tolerance. Hybridization ('Sparred Owls'). USFWS removal programs. The most significant interspecific conflict in PNW conservation. SPS degree 4 meets degree 137.

nodes.title: Range Expansion -- Eastern to PNW
nodes.description: Historical eastern species. Expanded via boreal forest corridor created by fire suppression. BC in 1940s-1960s, Washington by 1970s-1980s. Now abundant throughout PNW. Habitat generalism enables exploitation of diverse forest types including suburban areas.

nodes.title: Third Consecutive Strigidae (135-137)
nodes.description: GHO solo (135, E=4), GHO pair duet (136, E=5), Barred Owl (137, E=4). Three consecutive owl entries. The Strigidae era deepens with a new species. Fourth Strigidae in engine total (Spotted Owl at 4).

cross_references.relationship: ECOLOGICAL CONFLICT! Barred Owl (137, E=4) threatens Spotted Owl (4, E=3). 133 degrees apart. The expanding eastern species displaces the endangered PNW endemic. The most significant interspecific conflict in PNW conservation.
STRIGIDAE TRIO! GHO solo (135, E=4), GHO pair duet (136, E=5), Barred Owl (137, E=4). Three consecutive Strigidae. Different genera (Bubo vs. Strix). The GHO is also a predator of Barred Owls.
DOUBLE ENTRY PREVIEW! Measured hooting (137, E=4) to wild caterwauling (138, E=6). Fourth species with double entries. The most dramatic energy step in any double entry: E=4 to E=6.
